introSection: Tennessee Tornado is a steel roller coaster located at Dollywood in Pigeon Forge, Tennessee, USA. Opened on April 17, 1999, it was designed by Arrow Dynamics and is the company's last Custom Looping Coaster installation. The ride's track and supports were engineered by Ride Centerline.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
On June 30, 1998, Dollywood officially announced Tennessee Tornado with a statement from Dolly Parton. The Thunder Express station was reused for the new coaster. Dollywood hired Arrow Dynamics to build Tennessee Tornado.

The ride opened on April 17, 1999.

The ride was repainted for the 2025 season.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
When Tennessee Tornado was announced, Dolly Parton added that the ride would incorporate an element nicknamed the "iron butterfly". This is not a butterfly element, rather the vertical loop and sidewinder which combined resemble a butterfly. The roller coaster uses the more modern track style which Arrow Dynamics introduced in the 1990s. It also has large tubular supports, a departure from the lattice-style supports used by Arrow since the 1970s. The support structure is similar to that of Bolliger & Mabillard roller coasters and the Drachen Fire roller coaster at Busch Gardens Williamsburg.

Tennessee Tornado was one of the first Arrow Dynamics computer-designed coasters.

sections.title: Ride experience
sections.content: When the train exits the station, it makes a left turn and goes straight into a shed. Inside the shed, riders hear tornado sirens. The train exits the shed, makes a right turn and then climbs the tall chain lift hill. At the top, riders make a right turn and drop straight into a tunnel, reaching a max speed of. After the tunnel, riders head into a tall vertical loop. The train goes past a wagon and makes a right turn. Riders flip into a second vertical loop and a sidewinder, before going through several turns. After the turns, the train approaches the final brake run.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Tennessee Tornado has maroon track and supports.

fullText:
Tennessee Tornado is a steel roller coaster located at Dollywood in Pigeon Forge, Tennessee, USA. Opened on April 17, 1999, it was designed by Arrow Dynamics and is the company's last Custom Looping Coaster installation. The ride's track and supports were engineered by Ride Centerline.

On June 30, 1998, Dollywood officially announced Tennessee Tornado with a statement from Dolly Parton. The Thunder Express station was reused for the new coaster. Dollywood hired Arrow Dynamics to build Tennessee Tornado.

The ride opened on April 17, 1999.

The ride was repainted for the 2025 season.

When Tennessee Tornado was announced, Dolly Parton added that the ride would incorporate an element nicknamed the "iron butterfly". This is not a butterfly element, rather the vertical loop and sidewinder which combined resemble a butterfly. The roller coaster uses the more modern track style which Arrow Dynamics introduced in the 1990s. It also has large tubular supports, a departure from the lattice-style supports used by Arrow since the 1970s. The support structure is similar to that of Bolliger & Mabillard roller coasters and the Drachen Fire roller coaster at Busch Gardens Williamsburg.

Tennessee Tornado was one of the first Arrow Dynamics computer-designed coasters.

When the train exits the station, it makes a left turn and goes straight into a shed. Inside the shed, riders hear tornado sirens. The train exits the shed, makes a right turn and then climbs the tall chain lift hill. At the top, riders make a right turn and drop straight into a tunnel, reaching a max speed of. After the tunnel, riders head into a tall vertical loop. The train goes past a wagon and makes a right turn. Riders flip into a second vertical loop and a sidewinder, before going through several turns. After the turns, the train approaches the final brake run.

Tennessee Tornado has maroon track and supports.
